# VISUAL_RULES.md

# AIOS Visual Education Standard (VES)

## Purpose

This document defines the visual learning standards used throughout AIOS educational projects.

Visuals exist to improve understanding.

They are never decorative.

---

# Core Principle

If a concept can be understood faster through a visual explanation than through text, prefer the visual explanation.

---

# Required Visual Elements

Whenever appropriate, every chapter should include:

- Mermaid diagrams
- Workflow diagrams
- Process flows
- Comparison tables
- Timelines
- Real-world scenarios

Minimum recommendation:

- 2 Mermaid diagrams
- 1 Workflow
- 1 Comparison Table
- 1 Timeline or Process Flow

---

# Preferred Diagram Types

Use the diagram that best matches the concept.

| Concept | Recommended Visual |
|----------|--------------------|
| Process | Flowchart |
| Workflow | Flowchart |
| Timeline | Timeline |
| Collaboration | Sequence Diagram |
| State Changes | State Diagram |
| Git Branches | Git Graph |
| Architecture | Architecture Diagram |
| Decisions | Decision Tree |
| Relationships | Mind Map |
| Comparison | Table |

---

# Mermaid Standards

Prefer Mermaid over ASCII diagrams whenever GitHub supports the diagram type.

Supported examples include:

- flowchart
- sequenceDiagram
- timeline
- stateDiagram
- gitGraph
- journey
- classDiagram
- erDiagram
- quadrantChart
- pie

Use only the diagram types that improve understanding.

---

# Visual Placement

Whenever possible:

Visual

↓

Explanation

↓

Example

Avoid placing diagrams after long explanations when introducing a new concept.

---

# Comparison Tables

Use tables whenever comparing:

- Technologies
- Commands
- Features
- Concepts
- Advantages
- Disadvantages

Avoid large tables containing unnecessary information.

---

# Images

Images should:

- Teach a concept
- Be high quality
- Use relative paths
- Include alt text
- Include captions when appropriate

Avoid decorative images.

---

# Workflows

Workflow diagrams should describe:

- Development processes
- CI/CD pipelines
- Request flows
- Deployment flows
- Learning paths

Readers should understand the process without reading the surrounding text.

---

# Repository Structure

Reusable visual assets should be stored in:

assets/

├── diagrams/

├── workflows/

├── images/

├── icons/

Do not duplicate identical diagrams across multiple chapters.

---

# Accessibility

Visuals should:

- Work in light mode
- Work in dark mode
- Avoid color-only communication
- Use meaningful labels
- Remain readable when printed

---

# Final Principle

Every visual should answer one question:

"Does this help the reader understand the concept better than plain text?"

If the answer is no, the visual should not be included.
